problems.title: Single Underscore for Internal Use
problems.description: Create a `Car` class with an internal `_engine_type` attribute and a `start()` method. Inside main(), instantiate the class and print both the start message and the internal attribute to demonstrate the underscore convention. Implement the logic in main(...) and match the expected output for the provided cases.
problems.prompt: Create a `Car` class with an internal `_engine_type` attribute and a `start()` method. Inside main(), instantiate the class and print both the start message and the internal attribute to demonstrate the underscore convention.

problems.title: Single Underscore as an Ignored Variable
problems.description: Use `_` as the ignored loop variable in a `for` loop and print `Hello!` five times inside main(). Implement the logic in main(...) and match the expected output for the provided cases.
problems.prompt: Use `_` as the ignored loop variable in a `for` loop and print `Hello!` five times inside main().

problems.title: Double Underscore Name Mangling
problems.description: Create `Vehicle` and `Car` classes to demonstrate double-underscore name mangling. Print the parent identification value and the child-specific identification value to show that they stay separate. Implement the logic in main(...) and match the expected output for the provided cases.
problems.prompt: Create `Vehicle` and `Car` classes to demonstrate double-underscore name mangling. Print the parent identification value and the child-specific identification value to show that they stay separate.

problems.title: Dunder Special Methods
problems.description: Create a `Book` class with `__init__` and `__str__`. Inside main(), instantiate a book and print it so Python uses the custom string representation. Implement the logic in main(...) and match the expected output for the provided cases.
problems.prompt: Create a `Book` class with `__init__` and `__str__`. Inside main(), instantiate a book and print it so Python uses the custom string representation.

problems.title: Module __name__ Value
problems.description: Inside main(), print the module `__name__` value so the learner can see what Python sets for the current module. Use main() to produce the expected result shown in the example.
problems.prompt: Inside main(), print the module `__name__` value so the learner can see what Python sets for the current module.

examples.input:
Use this setup idea:
print the module `__name__` value so the learner can see what Python sets for the current module

notes: Automated tests are skipped because runtime module naming changes the output.
